_comment: Edit this file to update the live tracker. After saving, the site auto-rebuilds in ~30s. JSON syntax matters — every string in double quotes, commas between items, no trailing commas. Paste into jsonlint.com if unsure. To add a new course/faculty/club: copy an existing block, paste, edit fields. The 'interest' field MUST exactly match one of the keys in the 'interests' array.

meta.title: Stanford GSB Prep — Class of 2028
meta.subtitle: Built for Alexis · Course planning, faculty to meet, the 2-year roadmap

courses.title: Clean Energy Opportunities
courses.why: Cornerstone non-technical energy class. Business models, tech change, policy. Right starting point.
courses.description: Clean energy business models in the context of tech trends, market opportunities, and policy. Renewable economics, low-carbon markets, policy drivers.
courses.tip: Counts toward the Climate Solutions Initiative certificate. Mandatory attendance.

courses.title: Clean Energy Project Development & Finance
courses.faculty: Dan Reicher, Dave Rogers, Jeffrey Brown
courses.why: Project structuring, financing, deployment. Closest to your VC/operator wheelhouse.
courses.description: Reicher (former US Asst Secretary of Energy + project finance pioneer) teaches capital mobilization, project finance structures, and policy frameworks for clean energy infrastructure.
courses.tip: Rare access to someone who has pioneered renewable project finance. Best for climate VC, green infra, or policy paths.

courses.title: Sustainable Energy: Business & Policy
courses.why: Pairs strategy with regulation — useful for emerging-markets work.

courses.title: Environmental Entrepreneurship
courses.why: Scaling sustainable businesses. Project-based.

courses.title: Energy & Environmental Policy Analysis
courses.why: Quant policy lens; cross-register if you want depth.

courses.title: Stanford Climate Ventures (3-quarter)
courses.why: Project sequence with engineers + scientists; real deployment problems.

courses.title: Understanding AI Technology for Business Problems
courses.why: Best non-technical AI class at GSB. Anthropic / OpenAI guests. Translate biz → AI solutions.
courses.description: ML scientist (ex-Uber, ex-DeepMind) bridges AI tech to business problem framing. Hard part isn't building the model — it's articulating what AI can solve and why. Started January 2025.
courses.tip: Best for PMs, founders, strategists. Not for data scientists. Heavy practitioner visits.

courses.title: The AI Awakening: Implications for Economy & Society
courses.why: Brynjolfsson is THE economist of AI. Heavy guest lectures.
courses.description: Brynjolfsson's graduate course on AI's economic + entrepreneurial impact. Spring 2026 emphasis: venture creation using LLMs. Past guests include Eric Schmidt, Mira Murati, Jeff Dean.
courses.tip: Spring 2026 emphasis on AI startups makes it especially relevant for you. Also available on Coursera if you can't get in-person.

courses.title: Digital Marketplaces & ML Applications
courses.why: Project-based. Best class for applying ML to real biz decisions.

courses.title: AI-Powered Org / Future of AI in Work
courses.why: Newer GSB AI Lab series. Multiple short modules — pick 1–2.

courses.title: AI@GSB workshops + Applied AI Initiative
courses.why: Not a course — but the most practical applied-AI prep at GSB. Sign up week 1.

courses.title: Venture Capital
courses.why: THE VC class. Term sheets, fund dynamics, deal mechanics. Do not skip.
courses.description: Startup financing, VC decision-making, valuation, term sheets, fund dynamics. Strebulaev brings a decade of teaching + the Venture Capital Initiative research. Heavy guest speakers from top VCs.
courses.tip: ~1,000 students have taken this. Pair with Strebulaev's VC Initiative office hours and events.

courses.title: Formation of New Ventures
courses.faculty: Robert Siegel + practitioners (recommended by Alexis's GSB energy contact)
courses.why: Case-based with founders/CEOs. Prereq for STRAMGT 359.
courses.description: Founding from the entrepreneur's POV: opportunity assessment, founder fit, market timing, resource mobilization. Heavy founder visits.
courses.tip: Take in Y1 spring or Y2 fall if seriously considering founding. No exam — grade is participation + projects. Mandatory attendance.

courses.title: Aligning Startups With Their Markets
courses.why: Product-market fit deep dive. Rachleff = Wealthfront, Benchmark — exceptional teacher.
courses.description: Product-market fit deep dive. Distinguishing customer-articulation failures from true misalignment. Tactics for new vs. known markets.
courses.tip: Rachleff (Benchmark, Wealthfront founder) is a legendary product strategist. Rare practitioner access.

courses.title: Startup Garage
courses.why: GSB flagship hands-on. Build + test a startup with $1k stipend. Lottery — apply early.
courses.description: Two-quarter design-thinking lab. Teams design, build, and test new business concepts. 130+ Garage companies founded, $2.5B+ raised (DoorDash among them). Stefanos Zenios.
courses.tip: Commit to a real business idea, not a class project. Network + feedback are the real value. Apply early — fills fast.

courses.title: Lean LaunchPad
courses.why: Customer discovery / business model canvas. Cross-list — open to MBAs.
courses.description: Steve Blank's Customer Development methodology for deep-tech ventures. 10-15 customer interviews per week, weekly pitches with evidence-based results.
courses.tip: More disciplined than Startup Garage if you have a deep-tech idea. Expect heavy weekly homework.

courses.title: Launch to Liquidity
courses.why: Scale → exit playbook. Strong for VC investors who want to coach founders.

courses.title: Entrepreneurship Without Borders / Emerging Markets
courses.why: EM-specific lens. Confirm offering — varies by year.

courses.title: Entrepreneurial Acquisitions
courses.faculty: Peter Kelly, Gerald Risk, Jim Ellis
courses.why: ETA / search fund mechanics. The class that built the model. Mandatory if interested.
courses.description: Acquisition strategy, deal structure, valuation, post-acquisition integration from the buyer's view. Kelly co-founded a search fund, ran a healthcare acquisition, did 25+ deals.
courses.tip: Essential if you're considering search funds, bolt-ons, or CEO-via-acquisition. Take before S549.

courses.title: Managing Growing Enterprises (MGE)
courses.faculty: Peter Kelly, David Dodson, Coley Andrews, Kevin Taweel, Graham Weaver
courses.why: Founded by Irv Grousbeck — case-based scaling class. Multi-section. Other ETA must-take.
courses.description: Execution, scaling, operations for entrepreneurs post-PMF. 'How to get things done' in growing companies. Cap ~45.
courses.tip: Grade is 65% participation + 35% projects. Show up engaged.

courses.title: Search Fund Garage / Practicum
courses.why: For serious post-MBA searchers. Apply after S543.
courses.description: Intensive prep for launching a search fund post-MBA. Current searchers, CEOs, investors as visitors. ~25 students.
courses.tip: Only take if you're serious about searching post-graduation. Most graduates do launch within 1-2 years.

courses.title: Scaling Excellence
courses.why: Operational scaling — pairs with ETA stack.

courses.title: Marketing Management
courses.why: Distribution req in marketing. Foundation for any consumer play.

courses.title: Consumer Behavior / Frinky Science of the Mind
courses.why: Neuroeconomics of choice. Direct application to beauty branding (sensory + emotion).

courses.title: Brand & Storytelling courses
courses.why: Aaker's brand-personality framework underpins modern beauty positioning.

courses.title: Global eCommerce
courses.why: DTC + cross-border. Critical for any modern beauty brand.

courses.title: Digital Marketing Action Learning Program
courses.why: Project-based with a real brand client.

courses.title: Strategy in Technology Industries
courses.why: Burgelman is a legend — 40 years studying tech firm evolution. Foundational.
courses.description: Strategy, innovation, and competitive dynamics in tech-intensive industries. Burgelman is a towering figure in strategy research (40+ years).
courses.tip: For tech strategists, product leaders, corporate VC. Deep frameworks; old-school case method.

courses.title: The Industry / Industry of Industries
courses.why: Practitioner-led tech industry deep dives. Heavy case + guest based.

courses.title: Beyond Disruption / Innovation in Existing Orgs
courses.why: How incumbents innovate. Useful for search-fund-acquired companies.

courses.title: Platform Economics & Network Effects
courses.why: Marketplaces, ads, fintech. Best class for understanding modern tech moats.

courses.title: Technology Venture Formation / Tech Strategy
courses.why: Cross-register if you want engineering-flavored tech strategy.

courses.title: Leadership Labs
courses.why: Auto-enrolled. Take it seriously — feedback culture starts here.
courses.description: Mandatory Y1 fall. Hands-on team-based leadership practice with real-time coaching. Foundation for later OB work.
courses.tip: Quality of your reflection shapes the learning for your peers. Don't phone it in.

courses.title: Interpersonal Dynamics ('Touchy Feely')
courses.why: Most-recommended class in GSB history. Apply. Apply. Apply.
courses.description: Small T-groups (~12 students). Practice giving and receiving real-time feedback, observe how your behavior affects others, develop self-awareness through peer reflection and facilitator guidance.
courses.tip: Best for Y2s ready for emotional bandwidth investment. The most consistently top-rated course at GSB across 45+ years. Pre-qualification is via a Canvas assignment ~5 weeks before quarter start.

courses.title: Paths to Power
courses.why: Pfeffer's signature. Two sections, ~68 each, lottery. Read his book before applying.
courses.description: Pfeffer's case-based course on power dynamics, political skill, and influence in organizations. Includes guest speakers + a 'Doing Power' field project where you apply concepts in a real organization.
courses.tip: Counterintuitive but practical. Especially valuable if you tend to underestimate the role of politics in advancement. Read Pfeffer's '7 Rules of Power' before applying.

courses.title: Acting With Power
courses.why: Power through performance + behavioral psych. Pair with Paths to Power.
courses.description: Combines acting craft + psychology to build comfort across hierarchical roles. Develop 'characters' that perform effectively at different levels of power.
courses.tip: Pair with Paths to Power. Best for those uncomfortable in hierarchy or wanting executive presence.

courses.title: Arbuckle Leadership Fellows (program)
courses.why: Apply Y1 spring to coach Y1s in Y2. Free executive-coach training.

courses.title: Negotiations / OB core electives
courses.faculty: Frank Flynn, Lindred Greer, Margaret Neale
courses.why: Neale's Negotiations is must-take in Y1 or Y2.

courses.title: Sports Business Management
courses.faculty: George Foster, S. Hinkie, D. Kaval
courses.why: GSB's sports class. Open to all MBAs. League/team/agent/college lenses.
courses.description: League / team / athlete-agent / college economics. Revenue models, IOC governance, regulation, sports tech. George Foster + Sam Hinkie + Doug Kaval.
courses.tip: Mandatory attendance. Foster runs the Sports Management Initiative — connects to the Lacob/Ballmer alumni network.

courses.title: Datathletics: Data Analytics in Sports
courses.why: Tableau/SQL/Python for sports decisions. Light on prereqs.

courses.title: Sports Innovation Conference (March)
courses.why: Annual conference (13th in 2026). Volunteer to meet the speakers.

courses.title: Negotiations
courses.why: Neale is iconic. Useful for VC, ETA, and life.

courses.title: Global Management Immersion (GMIX)
courses.why: 4-week summer abroad. Use for EM VC connections (LatAm/Africa/SEA).

courses.title: Global Study Trips
courses.why: Short regional deep-dives. Cheap way to satisfy Global Experience Req.

faculty.name: Stefan Reichelstein
faculty.role: GSB · William R. Timken Professor (Emeritus, active)
faculty.teaches: Clean energy economics, carbon accounting, hydrogen, storage cost curves
faculty.why: Most accessible energy faculty for non-technical MBAs. Co-teaches Clean Energy Opportunities.
faculty.bio: William R. Timken Professor of Accounting and Faculty Research Director for the Steyer-Taylor Center for Energy Policy and Finance. Known for management accounting and cost analysis research. In recent years, focused on cost competitiveness of low-carbon energy solutions including solar PV, hydrogen, and carbon capture.

works.title: Corporate Carbon Emission Statements
Assessing the Costs of Industrial Decarbonization (2024)

faculty.name: Dan Reicher
faculty.role: GSB / Law / Steyer-Taylor Center · former DOE Asst Secretary
faculty.teaches: Project finance, federal energy policy, climate venture
faculty.why: Best person for project finance + EM angle. Goes way back in clean energy.
faculty.bio: Executive Director of the Steyer-Taylor Center for Energy Policy and Finance and Lecturer at Stanford GSB. Former U.S. Assistant Secretary of Energy under Clinton and Google VP for Energy. 25+ years across energy policy, law, finance, and technology.

works.title: Smarter Finance for Cleaner Energy: MLPs and REITs for Renewables
A Tale of Three Markets: California, Texas, Germany Renewable Experiences (2016)

faculty.name: Sally Benson
faculty.role: Doerr / Energy Sci & Eng · Precourt Family Professor
faculty.teaches: Carbon capture, geological storage, IPCC contributor
faculty.why: If you want to understand the technical realities of decarbonization at scale.
faculty.bio: Precourt Family Professor in Energy Resources Engineering at Stanford's School of Earth, Energy & Environmental Sciences. Director of the Global Climate and Energy Project; former Energy Division Director at the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (2021-2023). Lead voice on geologic CO2 storage.

works.title: Carbon Dioxide Capture and Storage: Issues and Prospects
The Role of Carbon Capture and Storage in Decarbonizing California's Electricity Grid

faculty.name: Arun Majumdar
faculty.why: Dean of Doerr; busy but engages with MBAs through Precourt events.
faculty.bio: Inaugural Dean of the Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability and Jay Precourt Provostial Chair Professor. Founding Director of ARPA-E under Obama; former VP of Energy at Google. Member of U.S. National Academy of Sciences and Academy of Engineering. Research on redox reactions for sustainable energy.

works.title: Nanoscale thermal rectification (Science, 2006)

faculty.name: Erica Plambeck
faculty.why: Teaches Environmental Entrepreneurship. Approachable, action-oriented.
faculty.bio: Charles A. Holloway Professor of Operations, Information & Technology at Stanford GSB. Expert in supply chain management and environmental sustainability. 2003 Presidential Early Career Award recipient. Focuses on aligning supply chain operations with climate and sustainability goals.

works.title: Engaging Supply Chains in Climate Change
Reducing Greenhouse Gas Emissions Through Operations and Supply Chain Management (2012)

faculty.name: Jim Sweeney
faculty.why: Teaches MS&E energy policy class — open to MBAs.
faculty.bio: Professor of Management Science and Engineering at Stanford. Senior Fellow at SIEPR, Hoover Institution, and Precourt Institute for Energy. 40+ years studying energy economics, electricity markets, and climate policy. Former director of Precourt Energy Efficiency Center.

works.title: Energy Efficiency: Building a Clean, Secure Economy
California Electricity Crisis (Hoover Press, 2002)

faculty.name: Erik Brynjolfsson
faculty.role: GSB / HAI · Director Stanford Digital Economy Lab
faculty.teaches: Economics of AI, productivity, future of work
faculty.why: THE economist of generative AI. Heavy industry network.
faculty.bio: Jerry Yang and Akiko Yamazaki Professor and Director of the Stanford Digital Economy Lab. Former MIT Sloan professor (1990-2020) and Director of the MIT Digital Business Initiative. Author of nine books and 100+ academic articles on IT productivity. Leading Transformative AI research at Stanford.

works.title: The Second Machine Age (NYT bestseller)
Machine, Platform, Crowd: Harnessing Our Digital Future

faculty.name: Susan Athey
faculty.teaches: ML / causal inference applied to digital marketplaces
faculty.why: Best applied-ML teacher at GSB. Project-based pedagogy. National Academy member.
faculty.bio: The Economics of Technology Professor at Stanford GSB. 2023 President of the American Economic Association. Former Chief Economist at U.S. DOJ Antitrust Division (2022-2024). First female winner of the John Bates Clark Medal. Research on digitization economics, marketplace design, and ML/econometrics intersection.

works.title: Economists (and Economics) in Tech Companies (JEP 2019)
The Impact of Consumer Multi-homing on Advertising Markets (Mgmt Science 2018)

faculty.name: Fei-Fei Li
faculty.why: Hard to get time with, but HAI events and lectures are open. Aim for those.
faculty.bio: Sequoia Capital Professor of Computer Science and Senior Fellow at Stanford HAI; courtesy appointment at GSB. Co-Director of Stanford HAI. Former VP at Google and Chief Scientist of AI/ML at Google Cloud. 300+ peer-reviewed publications, 346,000+ citations. Co-led ImageNet, foundational to modern deep learning.

works.title: ImageNet: A Large-Scale Hierarchical Image Database (2009)
Rapid Natural Scene Categorization in the Near Absence of Attention (PNAS 2002)

faculty.name: Ilya Strebulaev
faculty.role: GSB · David S. Lobel Professor; Director, VC Initiative
faculty.teaches: Venture finance, fund dynamics, private market data
faculty.why: VC class instructor. EM-friendly — has worked on emerging market VC research.
faculty.bio: David S. Lobel Professor of Private Equity and Professor of Finance at Stanford GSB. Founder and Director of the Stanford Venture Capital Initiative. Winner of the First Paper Prize in Journal of Finance and Fama-DFA Prize. MBA teaching award recipient.

works.title: Interim Valuations, Predictability, and Outcomes in Private Equity
Squaring Venture Capital Valuations with Reality
The Venture Mindset (book, 2024)

faculty.name: Andy Rachleff
faculty.why: Maybe the best practitioner-teacher at GSB. Office hours fill fast.
faculty.bio: Lecturer at Stanford GSB. Co-founder of Benchmark Capital (1995) and Wealthfront. Coined the term "product-market fit." Teaches courses on innovation, non-founder CEOs, and aligning startups with their markets.

works.title: Andy Rachleff on Democratizing Investment
Wealthfront case study

faculty.name: Bill Barnett
faculty.teaches: Strategy, market dynamics, the 'Red Queen' framework
faculty.why: Co-teaches with Rachleff. Sharp strategic mind.
faculty.bio: Thomas M. Siebel Professor of Business Leadership, Strategy, and Organizations at Stanford GSB. Chair of Environmental Social Sciences at the Doerr School of Sustainability. Directs the Stanford Initiative on Business and Environmental Sustainability. Studies organizational adaptation — known for the "Red Queen" framework on competitive evolution.

works.title: Red Queen research on organizational competition

faculty.name: Hayagreeva 'Huggy' Rao
faculty.why: Author of Scaling Up Excellence + The Friction Project. Warm, generous.
faculty.bio: Atholl McBean Professor of Organizational Behavior and Human Resources at Stanford GSB; Professor of Sociology (by courtesy). Studies social and cultural causes of organizational change and scaling. Co-director of GSB's Customer-Focused Innovation Executive Program.

works.title: Scaling Up Excellence (WSJ bestseller, with Bob Sutton)
The Friction Project: How Smart Leaders Make the Right Things Easier (2024)

faculty.name: Theresia Gouw
faculty.why: Co-teaches VC with Strebulaev. Useful network bridge.
faculty.bio: Co-founder of Acrew Capital. Stanford GSB MBA '94. Former Managing Partner at Accel Partners (first female partner) and Aspect Ventures. Forbes 100 Most Powerful Women; Forbes Midas List 9 times; Time's 40 Most Influential Minds in Tech. Co-teaches the VC course with Ilya Strebulaev.

works.title: Co-teaches Venture Capital course at Stanford GSB

faculty.name: Peter Kelly
faculty.role: GSB Lecturer · search fund operator (25+ acquisitions)
faculty.why: THE search fund teacher today. Office hours = priority access for searchers.
faculty.bio: Lecturer at Stanford GSB; teaches Entrepreneurial Acquisition, Managing Growing Enterprises, and Search Fund Garage. Three-time Stanford grad (BA, MS, MBA). Former CEO of Pacific Pulmonary Services (acquired via search fund he co-founded, 1993-2010). Invested in 60+ search-acquired companies; serves on 16 boards. Author of the Stanford Search Fund Studies.

works.title: 2024 Search Fund Study
2022 Search Fund Study: Selected Observations

faculty.name: Irv Grousbeck
faculty.teaches: Operating, ownership, leadership at growing enterprises
faculty.why: Living legend of GSB ETA. Verify availability in 2026.
faculty.bio: MBA Class of 1980 Adjunct Professor of Management. Co-founder and co-director of the Center for Entrepreneurial Studies (1996, with Charles Holloway). Founded Continental Cablevision in 1964. Principal owner of the Boston Celtics. Living legend of GSB ETA / search fund.

works.title: New Business Ventures and the Entrepreneur (textbook)

faculty.name: David Dodson
faculty.why: Dodson's MGE section is highly rated. Practical playbooks.
faculty.bio: Lecturer in Management at Stanford GSB. Stanford grad (Econ '83, MBA '87). Raised three search funds and served as CEO/Executive Chairman in tech and telecom. Co-founded Project Healthy Children (later Sanku) — grain fortification tech named in Time's 100 Best Inventions.

works.title: The Manager's Handbook (WSJ + USA Today bestseller)
Professor Dodson teaching case

faculty.name: Graham Weaver
faculty.why: 2024 Distinguished Teaching Award. Inspiring.
faculty.bio: Stanford GSB MBA '99. Lecturer at Stanford GSB; 2024 MBA Distinguished Teaching Award winner for Managing Growing Enterprises. Founder and Partner of Alpine Investors — a PeopleFirst PE firm investing in software and services for 20+ years. Pioneered the CEO-in-Residence talent model.

works.title: Alpine Investors case study

faculty.name: Coley Andrews
faculty.role: GSB Lecturer · Co-founder Pacific Lake Partners
faculty.why: Investor side of ETA — meet if raising a search fund.
faculty.bio: Lecturer at Stanford GSB; teaches Managing Growing Enterprises and Managerial Skills. Dartmouth + Stanford MBA. Co-founder and Managing Partner of Pacific Lake Partners (founded 2009) — invests exclusively in the search fund model; partnered with 200+ search fund entrepreneurs. Former Golden Gate Capital and Parthenon Group.

works.title: Coley Andrews teaching case

faculty.name: Jennifer Aaker
faculty.why: Foundational frameworks for any consumer brand builder.
faculty.bio: General Atlantic Professor at Stanford GSB. Pioneering behavioral scientist on how purpose, meaning, time perception, and storytelling drive performance and well-being. Award-winning research featured across top journals; books translated into 20+ languages.

works.title: The Dragonfly Effect
Humor, Seriously: Why Humor Is A Secret Weapon in Business

faculty.name: Baba Shiv
faculty.why: His Frinky Science class is a cult favorite. Direct cosmetics relevance.
faculty.bio: Sanwa Bank, Ltd. Professor of Marketing at Stanford GSB. Renowned behavioral economist specializing in neuroeconomics — how the emotional brain shapes decisions. Applies neuroeconomic insights to innovation and entrepreneurial leadership across Silicon Valley startups and Fortune 500.

works.title: Brain Activity Forecasts Video Engagement (PNAS 2020)
The Role of Hedonic Behavior in Reducing Perceived Risk (JCR 2005)

faculty.name: Pedro Gardete
faculty.why: Strong on DTC + targeted marketing.
faculty.bio: Associate Professor of Marketing at Stanford GSB. Empirical research on how peer influences shape consumer behavior, advertising strategy, and market outcomes. Showed that in-flight purchase proximity increases buying likelihood by 30% — pioneering work on social context in spending.

works.title: Social Effects in the In-Flight Marketplace (JMR 2015)
Tailored Cheap Talk: Privacy Policy Effects on Ad Markets (Mktg Science 2018)

faculty.name: Jonathan Levav
faculty.why: Award-winning teacher.
faculty.bio: Professor of Marketing at Stanford GSB. Two-time winner of dean's award for teaching excellence. Faculty director of the Behavioral Lab. Uses experimental psychology and behavioral economics to study how cognitive biases, emotions, and social influences shape consumer choices.

works.title: Virtual Communication Curbs Creative Idea Generation (Nature 2022)
The Asymmetric Impact of Context on Advantaged vs. Disadvantaged Options (JMR 2018)

faculty.name: Robert Burgelman
faculty.teaches: Strategy in tech industries, corporate evolution
faculty.why: 200+ case studies. Old-school sharp.
faculty.bio: Edmund W. Littlefield Professor of Management at Stanford GSB since 1981. Leading strategy scholar. His longitudinal field research on Intel's transformation over 30 years produced his seminal Strategy is Destiny. 2017 honorary doctorate from University of St. Gallen; PICMET Leadership in Technology Award.

works.title: Strategy is Destiny: How Strategy-Making Shapes a Company's Future (with Andy Grove)

faculty.name: Robert Siegel
faculty.role: GSB Lecturer · VC partner (Piva, XSeed) · author
faculty.teaches: Formation of New Ventures (STRAMGT 353), Entrepreneurial Finance, Strategic Management of Technology, The Industry
faculty.why: ★ Recommended by Alexis's GSB energy contact. Practitioner-teacher with 10+ courses taught at GSB; pulls phenomenal guest speakers. 2016-17 MBA Class of 1973 Lecturer Award.
faculty.bio: Lecturer in Management at Stanford GSB for 20+ years. Researches strategy and innovation across sectors. Authored 115+ cases (Google, PayPal, Stripe, SoftBank). Venture Partner at Piva Capital and General Partner at XSeed Capital. Teaches across entrepreneurship and tech strategy.

works.title: The Systems Leader (2024)
The Brains and Brawn Company (2021)

faculty.name: Scott Brady
faculty.role: GSB Lecturer (CES) · Founding Partner, Innovation Endeavors
faculty.teaches: Company creation, tech for construction & housing
faculty.why: ★ Recommended by Alexis's GSB energy contact. Brings real venture creation experience — three publicly traded tech co-founder; runs Innovation Endeavors (Eric Schmidt's VC firm). 2022 MSx Teaching Excellence Award.
faculty.bio: Lecturer in Management at Stanford GSB through the Center for Entrepreneurial Studies; Founding Partner at Innovation Endeavors (Eric Schmidt-backed VC firm). Has co-founded three publicly traded technology companies and serves on boards of category-defining startups. Brings operating + investing experience directly into the classroom. Recipient of 2023 Jack McDonald Military Service Appreciation Award and 2022 MSx Teaching Excellence Award.

works.title: Innovation Endeavors firm site

faculty.name: Charles O'Reilly
faculty.role: GSB · Frank E. Buck Professor (Emeritus, active)
faculty.teaches: Org culture, ambidexterity, innovation in incumbents
faculty.why: Lifetime Achievement Award. Direct relevance to ETA-acquired companies.
faculty.bio: Frank E. Buck Professor of Management at Stanford GSB. Leading organizational scholar on leadership, culture, diversity, and innovation. Published 100+ papers and six books. Winner of Distinguished Scholar Award (Academy of Management) and Lifetime Achievement Award (Organizational Behavior Division).

works.title: Lead and Disrupt: How To Solve the Innovator's Dilemma (with Tushman)
Leadership for Organizations (2019, with Waldman)

faculty.name: Bob Sutton
faculty.role: Engineering / GSB courtesy · Center for Work, Tech, Org
faculty.why: Author 'Good Boss, Bad Boss', 'No Asshole Rule'. Approachable.
faculty.bio: Professor Emeritus, Management Science and Engineering at Stanford. Organizational psychologist and NYT bestselling author on leadership, innovation, and workplace culture. Co-founded Center for Work, Technology and Organization, Stanford Technology Ventures Program, and the d.school.

works.title: The No Asshole Rule (2007)
Good Boss, Bad Boss

faculty.name: Jeffrey Pfeffer
faculty.role: GSB · Thomas D. Dee II Professor
faculty.why: Read 'Power' and '7 Rules of Power' before approaching. Blunt and brilliant.
faculty.bio: Thomas D. Dee II Professor of Organizational Behavior at Stanford GSB since 1979. Leading organizational theorist; author of 16 books. His Paths to Power course is one of GSB's most sought-after electives. Has served on boards of several human capital management companies.

works.title: 7 Rules of Power (2022)
Hidden Value: How Companies Get Extraordinary Results (with O'Reilly, 2000)
Power: Why Some People Have It — And Others Don't

faculty.name: Deborah Gruenfeld
faculty.why: Acting With Power instructor. Pair with Pfeffer.
faculty.bio: Joseph McDonald Professor of Organizational Behavior at Stanford GSB. Social psychologist studying how people are transformed by organizational and social structures, with particular expertise in the psychology of power. Co-directs the Stanford Executive Programs for Women Leaders.

works.title: Acting With Power: Why We Are More Powerful Than We Believe (2020)
Power and Influence theory (with Keltner & Anderson)

faculty.name: Frank Flynn
faculty.role: GSB · Paul E. Holden Professor of OB
faculty.why: Often teaches the OB core. Strong researcher + teacher.
faculty.bio: Paul E. Holden Professor of Organizational Behavior at Stanford GSB (joined 2006). Studies employee cooperation, work group dynamics, and leadership emergence through experimental and field research. Has informed exec ed at Google, Microsoft, Goldman Sachs, McKinsey.

works.title: Are We Intuitively Cooperative or Selfish? (multiple journals)
Research on prosocial leadership (Academy of Management Review)

faculty.name: Margaret Neale
faculty.why: Negotiations is a must-take. Neale is THE name.
faculty.bio: Adams Distinguished Professor of Management Emerita. John G. McCoy-Banc One Corporation Professor (2000-2012); Academic Associate Dean (1997-2000). First woman to receive the Davis Award for lifetime achievement. 25+ years at GSB. THE name in negotiation research.

works.title: Getting (More of) What You Want (2015, with Lys)

faculty.name: George Foster
faculty.role: GSB · Konosuke Matsushita Professor; Sports Mgmt Initiative
faculty.why: Runs the only formal sports business class. Connects to Lacob/Ballmer alumni network.
faculty.bio: Konosuke Matsushita Professor of Management at Stanford GSB and Director of Sports Management Initiatives. Australian economist with prolific research in financial analysis, entrepreneurship, and sports business. Order of Australia (AM) honoree; honorary doctorate from University of Sydney; Australian Accounting Hall of Fame.

works.title: Sports Business Management: Decision Making Around the Globe (3rd ed, 2026)
The Business of Sports: Text and Cases on Strategy and Management

faculty.name: Roger Noll
faculty.why: Less course-active but unmissable for sports econ context.
faculty.bio: Professor Emeritus of Economics at Stanford and Senior Fellow at the American Antitrust Institute. Leading expert on antitrust, regulation, and the economics of sports and entertainment. Testified as expert witness in O'Bannon v. NCAA and Alston v. NCAA. Author of 17 books.

works.title: Sports Economics on Trial: Alston v. NCAA (2022)
Sports, Jobs and Taxes (1997)

roadmap.body: Read Search Fund Primer (CES); Pfeffer's 'Power'; Brynjolfsson's 'AI Awakening' essay; rough draft of your goals doc.
roadmap.notes: Reach out to CES + Doerr to introduce yourself. Connect with 3–5 Class of 2027 students in your interest areas.

roadmap.body: Year 1 Core (Accounting, OB, Strategy, etc.) + OB 374 Leadership Labs (mandatory) + Negotiations if available.
roadmap.notes: Don't pile on electives Y1 fall — settle in. Find 2–3 peer advisors. Sign up for Stanford Energy Club + Sports Mgmt Club.

roadmap.body: Year 1 Core finishing + 1 elective from VC (FINANCE 384) OR Brynjolfsson's AI Awakening.
roadmap.notes: Apply to Arbuckle Leadership Fellows in spring.

roadmap.body: Formation of New Ventures (STRAMGT 353); Lean LaunchPad (ENGR 245); apply to Touchy Feely lottery.
roadmap.notes: Decide: GMIX summer? Internship? Search trip? Confirm Global Experience plan.

roadmap.body: Internship OR GMIX OR independent search-fund prep / energy project.
roadmap.notes: If energy: shadow a project at a developer. If search: 8 weeks of prospecting + LP outreach.

roadmap.body: Touchy Feely (if won lottery); S543 Entrepreneurial Acquisitions; Clean Energy Project Dev & Finance (Reicher); Burgelman Strategy in Tech.
roadmap.notes: Heaviest quarter. Apply for Paths to Power for winter.

roadmap.body: FINANCE 384 Venture Capital (if not done); Susan Athey ML/Marketplaces; OR Pfeffer Paths to Power; Clean Energy Opportunities (Reichelstein).
roadmap.notes: Run a small AI pilot in your Startup Garage / search target this quarter.

roadmap.body: Acting With Power (Gruenfeld); S549 Search Fund Garage OR Environmental Entrepreneurship; Sports Business Mgmt (curiosity slot).
roadmap.notes: Last chance for high-touch electives. Don't waste it on filler.

roadmap.body: Search fund / energy project / VC partnership.
roadmap.notes: Use CES + Doerr alumni networks aggressively. They're built for this.

advisorQs.q: What's the realistic course load per quarter? When do most people front-load vs. back-load?
Which Distribution Requirement classes are over-subscribed and which can I take any quarter?
Which electives lose 50%+ of applicants? When should I apply for Touchy Feely / Paths to Power / Acting With Power?
Walk me through course bidding. How many points do I get? Is bidding strategy a thing here?
Realistically, can I add the MS Environment & Resources (E-IPER) joint degree after I arrive, or do I need to apply now?
Is the new GSB Applied AI initiative considered a 'certificate'? What does formal participation give me?
What's the standard prep timeline for someone planning to launch a search fund right after graduation?
advisorQs.who: Peter Kelly / CES Search Fund team

advisorQs.q: I'm a non-technical investor interested in project development. Should I prioritize Doerr classes or GSB energy electives?
advisorQs.who: Dan Reicher / Precourt MBA contact / CES energy advisor

advisorQs.q: What classes touch emerging market VC, and which professors actively research it?
Are there GSB faculty/lecturers from beauty, or alumni who'd guest-lecture? Who runs Retail & Luxury Club?
Beyond GSBGEN 360, is there a way to do a Stanford Athletics-side independent study or project?
Which Global Study Trips are best for VC + emerging markets? (LatAm? Africa? SEA?)
Can I be assigned a career advisor who specializes in (a) VC, (b) ETA, and (c) climate tech? Or do I have to pick one?
What's the etiquette for cold-emailing a faculty member I haven't taken a class with? Lunch programs?
Can I run an independent study for credit, e.g. building an AI use case for a small business I'm scoping for ETA?
advisorQs.who: Peter Kelly / Susan Athey / dept chair

advisorQs.q: Can I audit classes I didn't get into the lottery for?
How much time off campus is realistic in Y2 (board roles, deal-sourcing, beauty brand consulting)?

resources.name: Center for Entrepreneurial Studies (CES)
resources.why: Home base for VC, entrepreneurship, search fund. Curates 50+ courses. Office hours, mentor pool.

resources.name: Venture Capital Initiative (Strebulaev)
resources.why: VC research, data, events. Get on the mailing list day 1.

resources.name: Search Fund Primer + 2024 Search Fund Study
resources.why: GSB's official ETA bibles. Read both before fall.

resources.name: Precourt Institute for Energy
resources.why: Energy seminars, fellowships, sustainable finance initiative. Cross-school.

resources.name: Doerr School of Sustainability
resources.why: Cross-register here for technical energy/climate classes.

resources.name: Steyer-Taylor Center for Energy Policy & Finance
Stanford HAI
resources.why: Best AI events on campus. Open to MBAs.

resources.name: AI@GSB / Applied AI Initiative
resources.why: Hands-on AI workshops, practitioner sessions. Your fast track to applied AI.

resources.name: GSB Sports Management Initiative
resources.why: George Foster's research + cases. Engages with Lacob/Ballmer alumni.

resources.name: Arbuckle Leadership Fellows Program
resources.why: Apply Y1 spring. Coach Y1s in Y2. Built-in executive-coach training.

resources.name: MS Environment & Resources (E-IPER) / MBA
resources.why: If energy is your THING, evaluate this in Y1 fall.

resources.name: GMIX (Global Management Immersion)
resources.why: 4 weeks abroad — perfect for EM VC connections.

resources.name: Explore Courses
resources.why: The actual source of truth for course offerings each quarter.

resources.name: GSB Academic Calendar
resources.why: Bidding/registration windows. Bookmark and live by it.

clubs.name: Africa Business Club
clubs.why: Most direct on-ramp into the Africa-focused community at GSB. Speaker series, treks (Lagos, Nairobi, Cape Town), case discussions. Bridges to Stanford Seed.
annualEvents: Treks to Lagos / Nairobi / Cape Town

clubs.name: Latin American Student Association (LASA)
clubs.why: Speaker series, career events, social glue for Latam community at GSB. Useful if you ever want to source/deploy in LatAm.

clubs.name: GSB Energy Club
clubs.why: Speaker series on grid, electrification, decarbonization, climate tech. Treks. Often the first place energy career intel lands.

clubs.name: Stanford Energy Club (cross-campus)
clubs.why: Bigger umbrella across Stanford — engineers, scientists, MBAs. Worth being a member of both this and the GSB-only club.

clubs.name: GSB VC Club
clubs.why: Pitch competitions, VC firm visits, panels with practitioners. Apply to leadership team in Y1 spring if you want a louder voice.

clubs.name: GSB AI Club
clubs.why: Bridges frontier AI development to business applications. Hosts builders from frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.).

clubs.name: Entrepreneur Club (E-Club)
clubs.why: First stop for any entrepreneurial interest at GSB. Founders' lunches, idea labs, demo days.

clubs.name: Search Fund / ETA community at CES
clubs.why: Not a formal 'club' but a tight community via CES. Search fund dinners, alumni searcher visits, mentor matching.

clubs.name: Consumer & Retail Club / Luxury Trek
clubs.why: Luxury Trek (Europe) visits LVMH, Cartier, prestige beauty. CPG and beauty exec speakers throughout the year.

clubs.name: High Tech Club
clubs.why: Tech career fair, recruiter events, treks to Bay Area tech companies. Massive — most popular career-track club.

clubs.name: Sports Management Club (GSB)
clubs.why: Annual Sports Innovation Conference, treks to teams + leagues, owner/exec speakers (Lacob, Ballmer network).

clubs.name: Stanford Sports Analytics Club (cross-campus)
clubs.why: Quant side of sports — analytics talks, datathons. Worth checking out for fun even if not your career.

clubs.name: Investment Management Club (Public Markets)
clubs.why: More public markets than VC, but solid speaker series and useful for understanding capital flows broadly.

clubs.name: Private Equity Club
clubs.why: Adjacent to ETA — many search funders engage. PE recruiting events too.

clubs.name: Real Estate Club
clubs.why: Useful if you ever look at infrastructure / RE-adjacent deals (data centers, solar farms, etc.).

clubs.name: Stanford Women in Business (SWIB)
clubs.why: Speaker series, mentorship, career programming. Open to all genders as allies.

clubs.name: Healthcare Club
clubs.why: Premier life sciences and healthcare industry club. Annual conference brings 1,000+ attendees including execs, clinicians, policymakers. Strong track record launching health tech ventures.

clubs.name: Black Business Student Association (BBSA)
clubs.why: Founded 1982. Support network for Black MBAs. Mentorship programs, annual gala, strong alumni connections. Open to all GSB students as allies.

clubs.name: Greater China Business Club
clubs.why: Build networks in Greater China region (Mainland, Taiwan, HK). Educational events on Chinese business, alumni connections, cultural mixers.

clubs.name: GSB India Club
clubs.why: Community for India-focused careers (VC, entrepreneurship, impact investing). Regular speaker events and networking.

clubs.name: South Asian Business Students Association (SABSA)
clubs.why: Community for South Asian students and those interested in South Asian culture/business. Works with ABSA for broader outreach.

clubs.name: Management Consulting Club
clubs.why: Prepares students for consulting careers. Case interview training, workshops, partner firm interactions. One of the most popular clubs at GSB.

clubs.name: Social Innovation Club
clubs.why: Innovative solutions to global social challenges. Connects with social innovation leaders, idea forums, career support.

clubs.name: FinTech Club
clubs.why: Financial technology landscape (banking, payments, insurtech, wealth management). Connects students with founders, investors, companies.

clubs.name: Women in Management (WIM)
clubs.why: Supportive community for gender equity in business. Strong network, executive speakers, annual banquet, career trek, leadership panels. Open to all genders.

clubs.name: Business & Environment Club
clubs.why: Cultivates environmental sustainability community at GSB. Climate Conference brings 500+ attendees. Active alumni network.

clubs.name: Arts, Media & Entertainment Club
clubs.why: Film, TV, music, publishing, new media. Members from MTV, Disney, Universal, Fox. Membership up 40% in recent years.

clubs.name: Product Design & Manufacturing Club (PDMC)
clubs.why: Bridges disciplines on product innovation. Design thinking applied to real-world challenges.

clubs.name: Asian Business Student Association (ABSA)
clubs.why: Unites GSB students from Asia, of Asian descent, or interested in Asia. Cultural integration, career exploration.

clubs.name: Pride Club (GSB Pride)
clubs.why: Social and socially active org for LGBTQ students and allies. Community building, awareness events, alumni networking.

registration.intro: Stanford GSB course registration is not first-come, first-served. It's a points-based bidding system run via Stanford's Axess platform. Save your highest bids for your must-have classes. Year 1 is mostly fixed core; Year 2 is where the bidding game gets real.

sections.title: How bidding works
sections.body: Every quarter you allocate bid points across the courses you want. All bids across all students are ranked highest-to-lowest, and the algorithm allocates seats in that order. Bid amount = how badly you want that specific seat. There's no benefit to logging in early — this isn't first-come-first-served. Spend your points strategically: heavy on your 3-4 must-haves, lighter on fallbacks.

sections.title: Super Round vs. Open Round
sections.body: High-demand courses (Touchy Feely, Paths to Power, Startup Garage) fill in the Super Round — the main bidding event. Most other electives fill quickly after. Lower-demand courses remain in an Open Round window where you can fill in the rest of your schedule. Some seminars also require short written justifications — these show up directly in Axess when you go to register.

sections.title: Famously oversubscribed classes — how to actually get in
sections.body: Rules of thumb for the most coveted classes:
items: Touchy Feely (OB 375): ~36 seats. Pre-qualify via Canvas assignment ~5 weeks before quarter start. Then waitlist/lottery. Bid high.
Paths to Power (OB 377): No prereq. ~2 rejected for every 1 accepted. Bid aggressively.
Acting With Power (OB 533): Limited capacity, lottery.
Startup Garage: Apply as a team of 2-5. Capacity ~48. Apply early.
Lean LaunchPad (ENGR 245): Team applications of 4-5 via leanlaunchpad.stanford.edu. Preference to grad students.
Search Fund (S543, S355, S549): S543/S355 open enrollment. S549 limited to ~25, requires application. Take S543 first.

sections.title: Year 1 vs Year 2
sections.body: Year 1 = mostly mandatory core courses. Limited elective space (1-2 quarters of breathing room). Year 2 = 100+ electives, distribution requirements, and the bid system runs hot. Most of the strategic game is Y2.

sections.title: Cross-registration (MS&E, CS, Doerr, Law)
sections.body: Cross-registration is allowed and common. You register through your school first; GSB deadlines are earlier than university-wide deadlines. Each school enforces its own capacity — some MS&E classes are open, others restricted to MS&E students. If you want a specific cross-listed class, email the instructor early.

sections.title: Strategy advice
sections.body: Things current and recent students consistently say:
items: Spend your bid budget on 3-4 must-haves; don't spread it thin.
Touchy Feely is genuinely worth the high bid — top-rated GSB course for 45+ years.
After Super Round closes, scan Open Round daily — good classes still appear.
Auditing or sitting in does NOT bypass the lottery.
TA-ing a course doesn't give you priority for other courses.
Don't leave Y2 spring empty — last chance for high-touch electives.

glossary.def: Stanford's online course management system. Where you bid and register.
The pre-registration lottery / bidding event for high-demand courses. Run before each quarter.
The fill-in window after Super Round, when lower-demand courses still have seats.
Scarce currency you allocate across courses to signal preference. More bid = higher priority.
Window after the quarter starts where you can change your schedule without penalty. Typically ~2 weeks in.
GSB's rule that you take one course in each major area (Accounting, Finance, OB, etc.) over the 2 years.
Some courses (notably Touchy Feely) require completing a Canvas assignment or short application before you can bid.

deadlines.note: First quarter — bidding happens in summer for incoming students

y1Core._note: Y1 Core list is approximate — Stanford GSB updates these slightly each year. Verify with Academic Services after admit.

Y1-Autumn.title: Microeconomics for Managers
Y1-Autumn.note: May be waivable with strong econ background

distributionAreas._note: GSB requires 15+ units across these areas (one course per area in Y1 or Y2). Mix of mandatory and choose-from-list.

advisorPrimer.intro: GSB doesn't assign you ONE academic advisor — you have a network of them. Each plays a different role. Don't wait for them to find you; the smartest move is reaching out in your first two weeks.

types.name: Career Management Center (CMC)
types.role: Your primary career partner. Customized career plans, job search strategy, network access, internship + full-time placement support.
types.when: Pre-arrival intro email; orientation; bi-weekly Y1 fall; ongoing throughout the program.
types.tip: Schedule meetings early and often. Update before recruiting seasons. Debrief after employer conversations.

types.name: Academic Services / Course Planning Advisor
types.role: Help align coursework to career goals. Guide core + distribution requirement choices. Walk you through bidding mechanics.
types.when: Available on-demand. Especially important during orientation and before each quarter's Super Round.
types.tip: Before registering for Y1 winter and spring, work with them to ensure your elective + distribution selections build toward your post-MBA plan.

types.name: Section Faculty Advisor
types.role: Faculty member assigned with your section in Y1. General mentorship, big-picture career conversations, recommendations.
types.when: Assigned at orientation. Lean on them throughout Y1; less frequent in Y2.
types.tip: Take their first office hour early so you're not a stranger when you need a recommendation.

types.name: Center Advisors (CES, Doerr, etc.)
types.role: Domain experts in your interest areas. CES = entrepreneurship + search funds. Doerr/Precourt = energy. HAI = AI. They unlock specialized networks.
types.when: On-demand once you've identified an interest area — typically Y1 spring or Y2.
types.tip: Don't wait for Y2. Reach out in Y1 fall if you know your direction. Early relationships unlock research projects, alumni intros, and founder networks.

types.name: Leadership Coach
types.role: 1:1 personal/professional development. Communication, executive presence, decision-making.
types.when: Often paired with Leadership Labs in autumn; available throughout.
types.tip: Underutilized. Especially valuable for non-traditional or international backgrounds. It's included in tuition — use it.

types.name: Peer Advisors (2nd-years)
types.role: Informal guidance on student life, workload, course intel, club picks, what's actually worth taking.
types.when: Introduced at orientation; ongoing informal access.
types.tip: Pick 2-3 from your section in your first week. Use peer advice for filtering and reality checks; use the formal advisors for strategic decisions.

timeline.what: Welcome email/call, MBA checklist, course registration prep, career goal inventory.
Meet academic advisor, CMC advisor, leadership coach. Squad formation. First advising meetings.
Bi-weekly CMC check-ins. 1:1 course planning. Leadership coaching starts.
Career exploration. Internship search. Distribution requirement planning.
Job search (summer internships → full-time offers). Elective planning. Specialization advising.

resourcesIntro: Stanford uses a few specific terms here. **Centers / Institutes** are faculty-led research and education hubs (CES = Center for Entrepreneurial Studies; Precourt = energy). **Initiatives & Labs** are smaller research efforts within or across centers. **Programs & Fellowships** are formal multi-week or multi-quarter programs you apply to. **Tools** are systems you'll use day-to-day (course catalog, calendar).
